coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of Cr(VI) via in situ encapsulation of green/red carbon quantum dots in zeolite

coredata.dc:description: To overcome the limitations arising from non-uniform particle size and solid-state aggregation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescence probe, GCQDs@SBA-15/RCQDs@SBA-15 (G-RCQDs@SBA-15), has been successfully constructed by simplifying the synthesis process through in situ synthesis of CQDs in SBA-15 to further enhance their optical properties for highly sensitive detection of Cr(VI). The probe G-RCDs@SBA-15, based on green-emission carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) encapsulated in mesoporous molecular sieves SBA-15, provides the response signal, while red-emission carbon quantum dots (RCQDs) encapsulated in SBA-15 serve as an internal reference. The probe exhibits a satisfactory limit of detection (LOD) of 0.336 μM, which is 52 % lower than GCQDs/RCQDs. Importantly, the probe shows superior selectivity in detecting Cr(VI) compared to other metal ions. The fluorescence signal ratio of the probe decreases with increasing Cr(VI) concentration, resulting in a distinct change of fluorescence color from green to red. Based on UV–vis and fluorescence lifetime analysis, Inner filter effect (IFE) is considered the primary potential mechanism for the fluorescence color transition of G-RCDs@SBA-15 following the addition of Cr(VI). G-RCQDs@SBA-15 not only inherits the excellent optical properties of GCQDs and RCQDs but also shows high fluorescence and chemical stability. Considering the sensitive photoconversion properties of G-RCQDs@SBA-15, a smartphone-based laboratory device in combination with RGB analysis software has been used for Cr(VI) detection by directly capturing and analyzing fluorescence images. In addition, the sensing system is validated in the actual lake water samples, exhibiting recoveries from 83.3 % to 96.3 %, indicating its excellent practicality for Cr(VI) detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of Cr(VI) via in situ encapsulation of green/red carbon quantum dots in zeolite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To overcome the limitations arising from non-uniform particle size and solid-state aggregation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescence probe, GCQDs@SBA-15/RCQDs@SBA-15 (G-RCQDs@SBA-15), has been successfully constructed by simplifying the synthesis process through in situ synthesis of CQDs in SBA-15 to further enhance their optical properties for highly sensitive detection of Cr(VI). The probe G-RCDs@SBA-15, based on green-emission carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) encapsulated in mesoporous molecular sieves SBA-15, provides the response signal, while red-emission carbon quantum dots (RCQDs) encapsulated in SBA-15 serve as an internal reference. The probe exhibits a satisfactory limit of detection (LOD) of 0.336 μM, which is 52 % lower than GCQDs/RCQDs. Importantly, the probe shows superior selectivity in detecting Cr(VI) compared to other metal ions. The fluorescence signal ratio of the probe decreases with increasing Cr(VI) concentration, resulting in a distinct change of fluorescence color from green to red. Based on UV–vis and fluorescence lifetime analysis, Inner filter effect (IFE) is considered the primary potential mechanism for the fluorescence color transition of G-RCDs@SBA-15 following the addition of Cr(VI). G-RCQDs@SBA-15 not only inherits the excellent optical properties of GCQDs and RCQDs but also shows high fluorescence and chemical stability. Considering the sensitive photoconversion properties of G-RCQDs@SBA-15, a smartphone-based laboratory device in combination with RGB analysis software has been used for Cr(VI) detection by directly capturing and analyzing fluorescence images. In addition, the sensing system is validated in the actual lake water samples, exhibiting recoveries from 83.3 % to 96.3 %, indicating its excellent practicality for Cr(VI) detection in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr) is a naturally occurring element commonly found in volcanic ash, rocks, and soil [1]. Chromium exists in various oxidation states, with the most common forms being trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) [2]. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is highly toxic, and humans can directly absorb Cr(VI) through contaminated groundwater or indirectly absorb it by consuming aquatic organisms (such as fish, shellfish, and seaweed) that have absorbed Cr(VI) from wastewater [3]. Cr(VI) can damage DNA [4]. Therefore, many countries strictly limit the concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water to below micromolar levels [5,6]. Rapid, selective, and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) is thus critical for water source monitoring [7]. Currently, analytical techniques for detecting Cr(VI) include colorimetry [8], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10], and inductively coupled plasma emission spectroscopy [11,12]. These methods provide accurate results and high sensitivity, but they often require specialized expertize, lengthy sample preparation, and laboratory conditions, which are not conducive to on-site detection [13], limiting their application in rapid Cr(VI) detection [14]. Therefore, reliable analytical methods are still needed to achieve rapid, accurate, and selective Cr(VI) detection.

In recent years, fluorescent probes have garnered widespread attention due to their high sensitivity [15], low cost [16], fast response, ease of operation, and strong real-time monitoring capabilities [17,18]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a material used in fluorescent probes, possess advantages such as a broad absorption range, high brightness, excellent chemical and optical stability, high water solubility, simple preparation, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, making them excellent fluorescence-based chemical sensors [19,20]. Gong et al. reported a CQD-based highly sensitive fluorescent probe for selective Cr(VI) detection with a linear detection range of 1.5–30 μmol/L and a detection limit of 23 nmol/L [21]. Liu et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs with strong blue fluorescence, showing selective detection of Cr(VI) with a detection limit of 0.26 μM [22]. However, these probes rely on changes in emission intensity at a single wavelength as the sole detection signal, providing an “on/off” response, which is susceptible to variations in probe concentration, experimental instrument parameters, and excitation intensity, leading to fluctuations in absolute signal intensity that may affect the accuracy of the results [20–26].

Dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensors generate two distinct fluorescence emission peaks under single-wavelength excitation. The concentration of the target substance is determined by the ratio of these two fluorescence intensities [27]. This method can effectively reduce potential external interference and introduce built-in calibration, significantly improving the precision and sensitivity of the analysis. Moreover, it allows for differentiation of the fluorescence intensity ratio at different emission wavelengths, and the fluorescence color changes under UV light irradiation as the target analyte or its concentration changes, making it visible to the naked eye. This visual detection method makes analysis and detection more efficient and convenient [28]. Zhang et al. developed a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe (Cr6P1) capable of sensitively and selectively detecting Cr(VI) under acidic conditions. As the Cr(VI) concentration increased, the emission at 502 nm from the Cr6P1 solution decreased, while the fluorescence intensity at 577 nm gradually increased. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F577/F502) showed a significant linear relationship within the Cr(VI) concentration range of 0–1 μM (R2= 0.997), with a detection limit as low as 13 nM (6.9 × 10−4mg/L) [29].

However, the aggregation of bare CQDs in the solid state can cause fluorescence quenching, surface defects, and a reduction in the effective area of CQD-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors, limiting their effectiveness [30]. To overcome the above issues, various solid carriers, including polymer matrices, inorganic salts, and porous materials, have been used to immobilize CQDs to create CQD-based nanocomposite materials [31]. Among these matrices, the mesoporous molecular sieve SBA-15, with abundant pore sizes, can provide a uniform reaction environment and ample growth space for CQDs [32]. Controlling the confinement of CQDs within the pores of SBA-15 improves particle dispersion and effectively prevents aggregation-induced quenching [33]. SBA-15, with a pore size of 2–50 nm, can accommodate large molecules that cannot be restricted by microporous materials, thus enriching the carbon source for producing CQDs. Additionally, SBA-15’s excellent optical transparency does not interfere with CQDs' excitation and emission. Based on these advantages, this study incorporated CQDs into the mesoporous material SBA-15, effectively simplifying multiple procedures such as CQD separation and purification [34]. Using SBA-15 to immobilize CQDs enables size adjustment of CQDs and achieves photoluminescence in the solid state. This process is relatively simple, conducive to large-scale CQDs production, and has significant potential in sensing, imaging, and optoelectronics.

In this study, an in-situ generation method of CQDs within the pores of SBA-15 was used. Compared to the traditional method of incorporating prefabricated CQDs into inorganic (organic) frameworks [35], this approach has the advantage of a simpler process while overcoming the photoluminescence (PL) quenching caused by solid-state aggregation of CQDs. Using the host-guest co-assembly concept, SBA-15 was selected as the host, and green and red CQDs were prepared in situ using L-cysteine (L-Cys) and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, respectively, to construct the G-RCDs@SBA-15 sensor. The sensor emitted two different fluorescence signals at 472 nm and 610 nm. The strong selective interaction between Cr(VI) and G-RCDs@SBA-15 led to a significant decrease in fluorescence at 472 nm, while the fluorescence at 610 nm remained relatively unchanged. The change in fluorescence color enabled ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of Cr(VI). Additionally, to simplify sensor application, a smartphone was used to quickly capture images, and the color change was quantitatively analyzed using a self-developed program, allowing portable, low-cost smartphones to replace bulky and relatively expensive fluorescence spectrophotometers ( Scheme 1).
Details about the reagents can be found in the Supplementary Material.

To prepare spherical SBA-15, 0.6 g of P123 was thoroughly dissolved in 12 mL of 2 M HCl under continuous stirring at 45 °C. To this, a solution of 0.1 g CTAB in 6 mL water and 5 mL ethanol was added. The resulting mixture was vigorously mixed at room temperature until the solution became clear. Subsequently, 2 mL of TEOS was slowly added dropwise over 30 minutes to the above solution while stirring at 40 °C. The resulting solution was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction vessel and heated at 80 °C for 5 hours, followed by a 12-hour incubation at 120 °C. The obtained solid product was calcined at 550 °C for 5 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min and a cooling rate of 5 °C/min.

0.3635 g of L-Cys, 3.3 mL of NaOH (1.0 M), 30 μL of H2O2 (30 wt%), 0.1 g of SBA-15, and 11.4 mL of ultrapure water were added to a 20 mL sample vial with a lid (the pH of the mixture was 9.50) and stirred at 90 °C for 12 h. The above whitish suspension gradually turned bright yellow. The resulting mixture was washed and centrifuged until the supernatant showed no fluorescence, then dried to obtain green fluorescent GCQDs@SBA-15, which was stored in the refrigerator. Similarly, 0.453 g of o-Phenylenediamine, 0.656 mL of HNO3, and 0.1 g of spherical SBA-15 were added to 30 mL of deionized water. The resulting mixture was stirred for 3 h, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to a reaction vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 200 °C for 2 h. The product was repeatedly washed and centrifuged until the supernatant had almost no fluorescence, then dried to obtain R-CQDs@SBA-15.
Details of the characterization methods used for GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 are provided in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 ratio fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: pH and reaction time were optimized to achieve the maximum sensing performance of the fluorescence probe for Cr(VI). Direct hybridization of GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 was carried out in deionized water (pH = 7.0) by adjusting the mass ratio of GCQDs@SBA-15 to RCQDs@SBA-15–4.5:1, establishing a ratio fluorescence probe G-RCDs@SBA-15 for the quantitative determination of Cr(VI).

A 1 M Cr(VI) stock solution was prepared using K2Cr2O7 for fluorescence sensing experiments. The fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) ions was carried out as follows: 1 mL of metal ion stock solution with a concentration of 500 μM (including Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, and Cr6+) was added to 5 mL of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 (control) solution at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. Afterwards, the photoluminescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded, where the maximum color change was observed for Cr6+, significantly surpassing that of other metal ions. All tests were conducted at the same room temperature. Different concentrations of Cr(VI) (0–600 μM) were then mixed under gentle agitation to explore the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 by Cr(VI) in a 5 mL control sample. The fluorescence color of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 gradually shifted towards red with the increasing Cr(VI) concentration.

The smartphone-assisted sensing device was constructed by connecting an iPhone 13 with 3D-printed accessories, including a sample chamber, a dark chamber, and an optical filter holder manufactured by a 3D printer. In a typical sensing experiment, a sensor suspension was mixed with varying concentrations of Cr(VI) in separate test tubes. Subsequently, test tubes were placed into the sampling chamber of the device, and corresponding fluorescence images were captured using the smartphone. The fluorescence images were then processed through RGB analysis software installed on the smartphone to obtain the corresponding RGB values to analyze the Cr(VI) concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The actual water samples were collected from the laboratory tap and the Songhua River. The water samples were filtered using a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane to remove insoluble impurities before use in the experiments. Cr(VI) standard solutions of various concentrations were then introduced into the filtered water samples. Subsequently, these solutions were added to a suspension of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 in deionized water. The precise amount of Cr(VI) was determined using the standard addition recovery method, and the fitted linear equation was employed further to assess the practical application value of the method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and TEM were first used to investigate whether spherical SBA-15 was successfully synthesized. The obtained SBA-15 exhibited a uniformly spherical shape, as evident from Fig. 1(a), with a diameter distribution ranging from 0.6 to 1.2 μm and an average diameter of 0.8 μm (Fig. 1g). The TEM image in Fig. 1(d) reveals a two-dimensional hexagonal P6mm structure of the mesoporous SBA-15 channels, displaying a highly ordered hexagonal array - a characteristic feature of this type of silica, consistent with the reported crystal morphology of spherical SBA-15 [36], indicating the successful synthesis of spherical SBA-15.

Novel composites GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 were successfully synthesized by introducing spherical SBA-15 during the synthesis of CQDs. SEM images of GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 are shown in Fig. 1(b) and (c), exhibiting a uniform small spherical morphology for both synthesized materials, with the diameter distribution range and average diameter remaining essentially unchanged (Fig. 1h and i). Fig. 1(f) and (g) display that the sizes of GCQDs and RCQDs loaded on SBA-15 are approximately 5 nm and 3 nm, respectively, and the lattice spacings are measured to be around 0.25 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene [37]. These observations collectively indicate the successful loading of CQDs into the interior of SBA-15 without causing alterations in its morphology and size. The Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was also performed to re-confirm the loading of CQDs in SBA-15. The EDS spectra of GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 observed on the Si wafer substrate are displayed in Figure S2 and S3, respectively, which confirm the presence of C, N, and O. Additionally, the EDS spectrum of GCQDs@SBA-15 exhibits a small amount of S. Thus, the above results verify the successful synthesis of GCQDs and RCQDs within the pores of SBA-15.

The small-angle X-ray diffraction (SAXRD) results of the synthesized spherical SBA-15, GCQDs@SBA-15, and RCQDs@SBA-15 are shown in Figure S4. In contrast to the traditional rod-shaped SBA-15, the spherical SBA-15 exhibits only a single (100) diffraction peak, indicating an ordered pore structure. Moreover, as the molar ratio of P123 to CTAB decreases, the d-spacing of the (100) plane decreases, resulting in a less pronounced (100) peak [38]. However, after the incorporation of GCQDs and RCQDs into SBA-15, this characteristic peak disappears, indicating the loss of the highly ordered mesoporous structure of SBA-15. The widespread distribution of GCQDs and RCQDs within mesoporous channels of SBA-15 likely leads to the disruption of this mesoporous ordered structure. Thus, the altered SAXRD spectra of GCQDs and RCQDs also confirm their successful encapsulation within the SBA-15 channels.

Furthermore, the specific surface area and pore capacity of different samples were also evaluated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm to explore whether GCQDs and RCQDs are confined within the pore space of spherical SBA-15. As delineated in Fig. 2(a) and (b), all samples exhibit distinct N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms within the relative pressure (P/P0) range of 0.5–1.0, indicating porous structures. Moreover, the desorption isotherms are situated above the adsorption isotherms, suggesting hysteresis effects and a significant population of mesopores (2–50 nm) [39]. These results further validate the successful synthesis of spherical SBA-15. Fig. 2(c) and (d) illustrate the changes in the pore volume of SBA-15 after introducing GCQDs and RCQDs; the detailed analysis results are presented in Table 1. Compared to pure SBA-15, the specific surface area and pore volume of GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 decreased, which may be attributed to the partial blocking of pores due to the introduction of GCQDs and RCQDs. This microstructural adjustment could potentially influence the optical properties and sensing performance of the materials. Overall, the SAXRD and N2 adsorption-desorption results indicate that GCQDs and RCQDs have successfully grown within the confined pore space of SBA-15, exerting an influence on both the crystal structure and pore architecture of SBA-15.

The synthesized GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 were further characterized through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The FT-IR spectra of SBA-15 after the introduction of GCQDs and RCQDs are shown in Figure S4. Broad absorption peaks at 453 cm−1 and 466 cm−1 can be observed in Figure S5 (a) and (b), attributed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si bonds, while prominent peaks at 777 and 795 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of Si-O bonds, confirming the presence of SBA-15 [34,40]. The FT-IR spectrum of GCQDs@SBA-15 displays characteristic absorption peaks at 3424 and 1124 cm−1, assignable to the O-H and C-O stretching vibrations of GCQDs, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of GCQDs [41]. The stretching vibrations of CO, CC, and C-N bonds are also observed at 1629, 1587, and 1407 cm−1, respectively, suggesting the existence of amide bonds in GCQDs, while the weak peak at 616 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of C-S [42,43]. Consistency between FT-IR and EDS elemental analysis results confirms the successful synthesis of GCQDs within the pores of SBA-15, with functional groups on its surface likely originating from the carbon precursor L-Cys.

Similarly, the FT-IR spectrum of RCQDs@SBA-15 also shows signals of several functional groups present on the surface of RCQDs derived from the precursor. As shown in Figure S4(b), the absorption peak at 3413 cm−1 corresponds to the deformation vibration of O-H groups in aliphatic alcohols, while the 2973 cm−1 peak is attributed to the out-of-plane deformation vibrations of aromatic C-H in substituted benzene rings [44]. The 1635 cm−1 peak can be assigned to the overlapping signals of CN and CC stretching vibrations, and 1495 cm−1 peak is attributed to the deformation vibrations of –NH2 or –NH– groups [45]. These results support the confined growth of RCQDs with amide bonds within the pores of SBA-15.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the surface composition of GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15. Their full-range XPS spectra ( Fig. 3(a) and (d)) confirmed the presence of Si 2p (103.08 eV), C 1 s (285.08 eV), N 1 s (401.91 eV), and O 1 s (532.1 eV), with GCQDs@SBA-15 additionally containing a small amount of S 2p (163.08 eV) [46,47]. The elemental pie charts shown in Fig. 3(a) and (d) reveal significant proportions of C, O, and Si elements, consistent with the results of EDS analysis. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of GCQDs@SBA-15 shows signals at 284.25 eV (C-C), 285.43 eV (C-N), and 288.6 eV (CO) (Fig. 3(b)) [48]. Similar signals are also observed in the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of RCQDs@SBA-15 (Fig. 3e)). The Si 2p bands for both materials (Fig. 3(c) and (f)) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 104.59 eV, 103.66 eV, and 103.2 eV, attributable to Si-N, Si-C, and Si-O, respectively [49]. The N1s XPS spectra of GCQDs@SBA-15 and RCQDs@SBA-15 exhibit three deconvoluted peaks at 399.22, 398.82, and 401.82 eV, which are assigned to C-N-C, N-O, and N-H, respectively (Figure S6) [50]. Hence, the XPS results are consistent with FT-IR analysis, corroborating the successful confined synthesis of GCQDs and RCQDs within the SBA-15 pores. Notably, GCQDs@SBA-15 exhibits a higher content of oxygen, nitrogen, and trace amounts of sulfur on its surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) using G-RCQDs@SBA-15
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent properties of the synthesized sensor materials were investigated in the aqueous solution using fluorescence emission spectroscopy. The fluorescence emission spectra of GCQDs, RCQDs, SBA-15, and G-RCQDs@SBA-15 are compared in Figure S7. The sensor G-RCQDs@SBA-15 displays two fluorescence emission peaks at 472 nm and 610 nm, similar to those of GCQDs and RCQDs, respectively. Contrarily, pure SBA-15 exhibited no significant fluorescence emission within the same range. These results imply that the fluorescence of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 mainly originates from two types of carbon quantum dots rather than SBA-15 itself. Moreover, these two emission peaks can be utilized as recognition and reference signals, allowing the construction of a ratio fluorescence sensor for subsequent detection experiments.

The sensitivity of Cr(VI) detection highly depends on the pH value of the reaction solution and incubation time, among other detection conditions. Noteworthy that within 4.0–10.0 pH range, the fluorescence intensity ratio difference ((F472/F610)₀−(F472/F610)) reaches its maximum at pH 7.0 (Fig. 5(a), where ((F472/F610)₀−(F472/F610) represents the fluorescence intensity ratio of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 in the absence and presence of Cr(VI)). The change in F472/F610 is rapid, reaching equilibrium within 30 s (Figure S8). Therefore, in the subsequent experiments, the analytical system was incubated for 30 s at pH 7.0. Additionally, the concentration ratio between GRCQDs and RCQDs plays a significant role in the detection of Cr(VI). As shown in Figure S1, within a 30-second incubation period, the difference in fluorescence intensity ratio ((F476/F615)₀−(F476/F615)) reaches its maximum value when the mass ratio is 4:1. Based on these results, the optimal detection performance for Cr(VI) in aqueous solution is achieved when the mass ratio of GCQDs to RCQDs is set to 4:1 in the ratiometric fluorescence probe.

Subsequently, the Cr(VI) detection capability of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 was investigated under the above-mentioned optimal conditions. As observable in Fig. 4(B), with the increased Cr(VI) concentration, the 472 nm emission of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 was significantly quenched, while the intensity of the 610 nm emission remained unchanged. Simultaneously, the color of the reaction system gradually changed from green to red, establishing a proportionate fluorescence probe that exhibits a sensitive response to Cr(VI). As depicted in Fig. 5(b) and (c), the relationship between F472/F610 and Cr(VI) concentration follows the traditional Stern-Volmer relationship [51]. F472/F610 shows a well-defined linear relationship with Cr(VI) concentrations in the 0–160 μM range (R² = 0.9998). Additionally, the detection limit is determined to be 0.336 μM, calculated using the formula 3δ/S (where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration graph) [52].

Meanwhile, the LOD of G-RCQDs is 0.646 μM, and after confining the G-RCQDs in the mesoporous molecular sieve channels, the LOD decreased by 52 %. This reduction in the detection limit may be attributed to the synergistic effects between the CQDs and the mesoporous molecular sieve: (1) The in-situ synthesis of GCQDs and RCQDs in the nanopores of the molecular sieve effectively reduces the solid-state aggregation of CQDs, thereby decreasing luminescence quenching effects, improving photostability, and lowering the detection limit; (2) The pores of the mesoporous molecular sieve significantly increase the surface area and adsorption sites. Compared to G-RCQDs, the adsorption capacity and removal rate of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 are more than twice as high (Figure S9) [53]; (3) The mesoporous molecular sieve protects carbon quantum dots from environmental influences such as oxidation and photodegradation, further enhancing their photostability and reducing the detection limit. In summary, the lower detection limit of the host-guest co-assembly compared to G-RCQDs may result from the combined effects of reduced solid-state aggregation, increased surface area and adsorption sites, and improved photostability.

Meanwhile, the LOD of G-RCQDs is 0.646 μM, and after confining the G-RCQDs in the mesoporous molecular sieve channels, the LOD decreased by 52 %. This reduction in the detection limit may be attributed to the synergistic effect between the CQDs and the mesoporous molecular sieve: (1) In-situ synthesis of GCQDs and RCQDs in the nanopores of molecular sieve effectively reduces the solid-state aggregation of CQDs, thereby decreasing luminescence quenching effects, improving photostability, and lowering the detection limit; (2) Pores of the mesoporous molecular sieve provides a larger surface area and more active sites, enhancing the fluorescence performance of carbon quantum dots and reducing the detection limit; (3) The mesoporous molecular sieve protects carbon quantum dots from environmental influences such as oxidation and photodegradation, boosting their photostability and reducing the detection limit. In summary, the lower detection limit of the host-guest co-assembly than the G-RCQDs may result from the combined effects of reduced solid-state aggregation, increased surface area and active sites, improved photostability, and signal enhancement.

A relative standard deviation of 3.2 % was obtained by performing fifteen sensing measurements of 15 μmol/L Cr(VI) using G-RCQDs@SBA-15 (Figure S10), demonstrating excellent sensitivity and reproducibility of the G-RCQDs@SBA-15 probe. This excellent performance is mainly attributed to the porous structure of SBA-15, effectively preventing the aggregation of encapsulated GCQDs and RCQDs in its pores. Moreover, the porous structure provides a larger specific surface area, increasing the contact area between the probe and the target and enabling sensitive detection of Cr(VI).

Finally, the selectivity and stability of the G-RCQDs@SBA-15 probe towards different metal ions were investigated. Different metal ions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3 +, Zn2+ , Ag+, Ni2 +, Co2 +, Cu2 +, Fe3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, and Fe2+ (1 mL, 500 μM each), were separately added to the G-RCQDs@SBA-15 probe at pH 7.0, and the changes in F472/F610 were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5(d), the F472/F610 ratio significantly lowered upon the addition of Cr6+ (50 μmol L⁻¹). However, no significant changes (orange bars) were observed in F472/F610 after adding other ions. Meanwhile, the absorption peak of Cr6+ precisely overlaps with the excitation wavelength of GCQDs at 365 nm (Figure S11). The excitation wavelength of GCQDs can be absorbed by Cr6+, which suppresses the fluorescence of GCQDs. These results indicate that G-RCQDs@SBA-15 exhibits selective fluorescence response to F472/F610 detection.

Moreover, adding Cr(VI) (2 mL, 50 μmol L⁻¹) solution to a mixed solution of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 and the aforementioned interfering ions caused a similar reduction of F472/F610, suggesting that the sensor possesses good interference resistance and can be applied for practical sample detection. In summary, probe G-RCQDs@SBA-15 demonstrates several advantages, such as ease of synthesis, low cost, high sensitivity, and good selectivity.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs can be classified into five categories based on the interaction between CDs and quencher molecules: dynamic quenching, static quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [54]. First, the fluorescence lifetime change of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 after adding Cr(VI) was analyzed to deduce the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescence lifetime of GCQDs@SBA-15 remained almost unchanged after the addition of Cr(VI) (from 0.66 ns to 0.31 ns), excluding the possibility of dynamic quenching and FRET [55].

Next, based on the UV-Vis absorption spectra of GCQDs@SBA-15 and Cr(VI) in Fig. 6(b), there is significant overlap between the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) at 400 nm and the excitation and emission spectra of G-RCQDs@SBA-15, indicating that IFE is likely responsible for the fluorescence quenching of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 caused by Cr(VI) [56].

To further explain the IFE mechanism, the percentage contribution of IFE to the overall quenching process was calculated using the Parker equation [57]. The fluorescence signal of GCQDs@SBA-15 was corrected using the following equation:

Table 2 lists the parameters involved in calculating the percentage contribution of IFE. As shown in Fig. 7, the results indicate that the percentage of IFE in the quenching of CNDs by Cr(VI) is as high as 80 %, suggesting that IFE contributes the majority of the overall fluorescence quenching process.

The stability of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 is a crucial factor in assessing its practical applications. A systematic study was conducted on the effects of time and other coexisting anions on the fluorescence stability of G-RCQDs@SBA-15. As shown in Fig. 8(a), after 6 weeks of storage under natural light, G-RCQDs@SBA-15 still maintained a high fluorescence ratio compared to pure carbon quantum dots (Figure S13). This is because the mesoporous molecular sieve protects CQDs from environmental factors such as oxidation and photodegradation, thereby enhancing the photostability of carbon quantum dots. Fig. 8(b) illustrates the effects of common anions on fluorescence stability. It was found that as the concentration of anions increased, the fluorescence ratio of G-RCQDs@SBA-15 solution was minimally affected, demonstrating good photostability and ensuring its suitability for detecting pollutants in natural water bodies.

A portable device utilizing an imaging-enabled iPhone X for imaging was used for Cr(VI) detection. During the imaging process, a miniaturized ultraviolet (UV) light generator with a wavelength of 365 nm and 100 mW power served as the excitation light source. Fluorescent images were captured using the smartphone's camera three seconds after adding the Cr(VI) solution. Integrating the ratio fluorescence sensor G-RCQDs@SBA-15 with a smartphone creates a potent portable device with high sensitivity and low background noise, making it suitable for on-site quantitative sensing of ions.

Considering the data processing capability of smartphones, our research group developed software capable of identifying RGB values in the images, as delineated in Fig. 9(e). The software was developed using technologies such as HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, which could control the smartphone to capture photo images and retrieve the corresponding RGB values. Combining a smartphone with G-RCQDs@SBA-15 provides a portable and cost-effective solution for ion detection, and the developed software makes it more convenient for on-site quantitative analysis.

Further, we demonstrated the practicality and accuracy of our portable detection method, which is of great significance for on-site quantitative detection. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the fluorescence color of the sensor at different Cr(VI) concentrations (0–600 μmol L−1) was captured with a smartphone, following the CIE (Commission Internationale d′eclairage, 1931) chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 9(b)). The relationship between G/R (green-red channel) and Cr(VI) concentration is depicted in Fig. 9(c). A good linear relationship (R² = 0.9998) between G/R and Cr(VI) concentrations (0–600 μmol L−1) was obtained (Fig. 9(d)) following a linear equation of G/R = −0.005X + 1.47099, with LOD of 0.320 nmol L−1 (X= concentration of Cr(VI), μmol L−1). The response of the sensing system to a Cr(VI) solution (3 μM) was blindly measured repeatedly, showing a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.11 % (N = 6), indicating its excellent reproducibility (Figure S14).

The linear range and LOD obtained from colorimetric detection are almost identical to those obtained from ratio fluorescence detection. Therefore, the results of Cr(VI) colorimetric detection demonstrate the practicability of this device, offering rapid (10 minutes) and easily readable results. Compared with other methods reported in the literature, our portable handheld device exhibits unique advantages in terms of detection limits and detection time. Hence, the handheld device developed in this work provides a strategy for on-site quantitative monitoring of Cr(VI).

To further validate its practicality,the smartphone-based detection platform was evaluated for monitoring Cr(VI) in real samples. The detection performance was assessed through recovery experiments by adding different concentrations of Cr(VI) to samples of lake water, tap water, and mineral water pre-treated with a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane containing the G-RCQDs@SBA-15 probe. Different Cr(VI) concentrations were analyzed using the smartphone application and are tabulated in Table 3. Recovery rates from the spike recovery tests were calculated to be in the range of 83.3–96.3 %, indicating the potential of the smartphone-based detection platform in various environmental sensing applications.

In this study, we utilized an in-situ synthesis strategy to prepare CQDs-in-zeolite to achieve controlled dispersion of CQDs within the pore space of SBA-15, enhancing the uniform distribution of CQDs particles and effectively preventing their aggregation-induced quenching. CQDs with different photoluminescent properties could be synthesized by adjusting precursor types and ratios in this versatile strategy, eliminating the need for additional separation and purification steps and facilitating direct application in processing, which is beneficial for large-scale production and industrialization. The ratio fluorescence sensing system based on green and red-emitting CQDs in SBA-15 was validated for the fluorescence and visual detection of Cr(VI). Its green fluorescence decreased in the presence of Cr(VI), while red fluorescence did not change, indicating the "on" state of the ratio fluorescence sensor. The successful integration of this probe with a smartphone color recognition application allowed for the conversion of imperceptible color changes into specific values, providing a practical tool for detecting and monitoring Cr(VI) in environmental samples. This comprehensive approach validates the promising application of this detection system in analyzing environmental samples.
